stem: A 58-year-old man with decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C, elevated INR, ascites, mild encephalopathy) is admitted with anxiety and early alcohol-withdrawal symptoms and needs an oral benzodiazepine. Because his hepatic oxidative capacity is severely impaired, you want the agent least likely to accumulate — one cleared by direct glucuronidation with no oxidative CYP step and no active metabolite. Which is the best choice?
explanation: Lorazepam is conjugated by glucuronidation (UGT) to an inactive glucuronide with NO oxidative CYP step and no active metabolite, so its clearance is largely preserved in liver disease — one of the LOT benzodiazepines (Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam) preferred in hepatic impairment. Diazepam is the classic withdrawal agent but is oxidized (CYP2C19/3A4) to long-lived ACTIVE metabolites (nordiazepam, half-life up to ~200 h) that accumulate dangerously when hepatic oxidation fails. Chlordiazepoxide, another traditional withdrawal drug, is oxidatively metabolized to multiple active metabolites (including desmethyldiazepam) that likewise accumulate. Clonazepam depends on hepatic nitro-reduction and CYP3A4, so its clearance is also reduced in significant liver disease.
focus: LOT benzos safe in liver disease (lorazepam/oxazepam/temazepam) — glucuronidation, no active metabolite

stem: A 34-year-old woman with generalized anxiety disorder and a history of sedative and alcohol use disorder (now in recovery) needs long-term pharmacotherapy for chronic daily worry. She is worried about the abuse potential of prior treatments. You want a non-GABAergic anxiolytic with no dependence liability that must be taken on a fixed daily schedule — its benefit builds over weeks and it is ineffective when used as-needed. Which agent fits?
explanation: Buspirone is a 5-HT1A partial agonist with NO GABA-A/benzodiazepine activity, so it is non-sedating and non-dependence-forming; its anxiolytic effect builds over 1-2 weeks (full effect ~4 weeks), so it is dosed regularly BID-TID and is NOT effective PRN or for acute anxiety. Alprazolam, lorazepam, and diazepam are all GABA-A benzodiazepines (targeted substances) with dependence liability — alprazolam is the worst choice here given its rapid onset, short half-life, and prominent interdose rebound, exactly the reinforcement profile to avoid in a patient with sedative use disorder.
focus: non-dependence-forming anxiolytic (buspirone) — 5-HT1A partial agonist, delayed onset, scheduled not PRN

stem: A patient with panic disorder is maintained on a short-half-life, high-potency benzodiazepine dosed three times daily but reports 'clock-watching' and rebound anxiety in the hours before each dose. You want to switch to an agent of similar high potency but with a LONG half-life and no significant active metabolite, so plasma levels stay steady and interdose rebound is minimized. Which is the best replacement?
explanation: Clonazepam is a high-potency benzodiazepine with a long half-life (~18-50 h) and no significant active metabolite, giving stable plasma levels that smooth out the interdose rebound seen with short-acting, high-potency agents like alprazolam. Oxazepam is short-acting (~5-15 h) and low-potency, so it would not relieve the rebound problem. Triazolam (~1.5-5.5 h) and midazolam (~1.5-3 h) are ultra-short-acting and would markedly WORSEN interdose rebound; both are sleep-onset/sedation agents, not maintenance anxiolytics.
focus: half-life/duration — long half-life smooths interdose rebound in panic disorder

stem: A 62-year-old with chronic insomnia marked by frequent middle-of-the-night awakenings (a sleep-MAINTENANCE problem, not just trouble falling asleep) has a prior history of hypnotic misuse and wants a non-controlled agent that is not dependence-forming yet improves BOTH sleep onset and maintenance. Which mechanism best matches these requirements?
explanation: Daridorexant is a dual orexin receptor antagonist (DORA) that blocks OX1R/OX2R wake-promoting signalling, improving both sleep ONSET and MAINTENANCE; it is not a controlled substance, is non-dependence-forming, and its short half-life (~8 h) minimizes next-day impairment. Zolpidem (an alpha-1-selective imidazopyridine Z-drug, targeted substance) and zopiclone (a cyclopyrrolone Z-drug) both act at the benzodiazepine site and are dependence-forming — precisely the liability this patient wants to avoid — and zolpidem's very short half-life addresses onset more than maintenance. Ramelteon is a melatonergic MT1/MT2 agonist that is non-dependence-forming but targets sleep ONSET only, so it does not address the maintenance component.
focus: orexin antagonist (DORA) vs Z-drug vs melatonergic — non-dependence + sleep maintenance

stem: An anesthesiologist needs a benzodiazepine for conscious sedation immediately before a brief endoscopic procedure. The requirements: intravenous administration, onset within 1-3 minutes, an ultra-short duration so the patient recovers quickly afterward, and clinically useful anterograde amnesia. Which agent best meets all of these needs?
explanation: Midazolam is an ultra-short-acting benzodiazepine (half-life ~1.5-3 h) available parenterally (IV/IM), with very rapid IV onset (1-3 min) and useful anterograde amnesia — the standard procedural-sedation agent. Diazepam also has rapid IV onset but a LONG effective half-life via long-lived active metabolites (nordiazepam up to ~200 h), causing prolonged sedation and slow recovery. Clonazepam is long-acting and has no parenteral formulation suited to this use. Temazepam is an oral hypnotic (glucuronidated, ~8-15 h) with no IV route and slower onset — unsuitable for acute procedural sedation.
focus: procedural sedation — ultra-short parenteral benzodiazepine (midazolam) with rapid onset and amnesia
